schools.summary: Gill: the same is observed before, referring to the times before the judges; a Levite took a concubine out of Bethlehem-judah.

themes: in those days, when there was no king in Israel, that there was a certain Levite... who took to him a concubine
the want of godly rule breeding corruption through every order
every man did that which was right in his own eyes (Judg. 21:25)

schools.summary: Gill: she was unfaithful, breaking the covenant; or she was angry and left him; was there four whole months.

debates: Did the concubine play the harlot or merely leave in anger?

The witnesses note the Hebrew is read two ways: that she 'played the whore against him' (the AV, infidelity), or that she was angry and left him (so the LXX and some), the verb being capable of meaning to be offended. Gill notes both readings; either way the union was broken.
themes: his concubine played the whore against him, and went away from him unto her father's house
the fruit of an alliance not according to God's order, prone to breach
they have dealt treacherously against the LORD (Hos. 5:7)

schools.summary: Gill: he arose from the place where he lived to go after her.
Clarke: he rejoiced, hoping to reconcile his daughter and her husband.

themes: her husband arose, and went after her, to speak friendly unto her, and to bring her again
the way of grace that pursues the wanderer to restore
the Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost (Luke 19:10)

schools.summary: Gill: he prevailed upon him to stay some time with him.
Keil & Delitzsch: him abiding three days, eating, drinking, and lodging.

themes: his father in law... retained him; and he abode with him three days: so they did eat and drink, and lodged there
the grace of hospitality, receiving and refreshing one another
distributing to the necessity of saints; given to hospitality (Rom. 12:13)

schools.summary: Gill: the time the Levite had agreed to stay being up.
Ellicott: most journeys were performed in the early morning.

themes: Comfort thine heart with a morsel of bread, and afterward go your way
using even lawful comforts with discretion, lest delay lead into trouble
redeeming the time, because the days are evil (Eph. 5:16)

schools.summary: Gill: they sat down at table, the Levite having risen to go.
Keil & Delitzsch: the father said, tarry all night, and let thine heart be merry.

themes: Be content, I pray thee, and tarry all night, and let thine heart be merry
excess of hospitality and mirth keeping a man from his duty and way
as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of the fool (Eccl. 7:6)

schools.summary: Gill: he rose up from table to depart and proceed on his journey.
Ellicott: his father-in-law urged him to stay.

themes: when the man rose up to depart, his father in law urged him: therefore he lodged there again
holding to right resolutions, not lightly moved from one's course
be ye stedfast, unmoveable, always abounding in the work of the Lord (1 Cor. 15:58)

schools.summary: Gill: he rose with a full intention to take his leave and be gone.
Keil & Delitzsch: them tarrying until afternoon.

themes: he arose early in the morning on the fifth day to depart... And they tarried until afternoon, and they did eat both of them
a course of small yieldings and lost time opening the door to trouble
walk while ye have the light, lest darkness come upon you (John 12:35)

schools.summary: Gill: they rose up, having refreshed themselves comfortably.
Keil & Delitzsch: the father urging them to tarry, the day declining.

themes: the day draweth toward evening, I pray you tarry all night
the accumulated delays of ease forcing travel at a perilous hour
the prudent man looketh well to his going (Prov. 14:15)

schools.summary: Gill: he was determined on his journey, seeing no end to the solicitations; came over against Jebus, which is Jerusalem.

themes: the man would not tarry that night, but he rose up and departed, and came over against Jebus, which is Jerusalem
the consequences of delay pressing upon a man; the wise order their goings in season
to every thing there is a season, and a time to every purpose (Eccl. 3:1)

schools.summary: Gill: the sun was going down apace and near setting.
Clarke: Jebus was Jerusalem, then held by the Jebusites.

themes: when they were by Jebus, the day was far spent; and the servant said... let us turn in into this city of the Jebusites
the strait of a lost day, pressed for shelter as night falls
the night cometh, when no man can work (John 9:4)

schools.summary: Gill: he would not turn into a city of the Gentiles, the Jebusites being of the seven nations; chose to pass over to Gibeah.

themes: We will not turn aside hither into the city of a stranger, that is not of the children of Israel; we will pass over to Gibeah
the corruption of a professing people sinking below the heathen
such fornication as is not so much as named among the Gentiles (1 Cor. 5:1)

schools.summary: Gill: he urged them to get on as fast as they could.
Ellicott: Ramah was only two miles beyond Gibeah.

themes: let us draw near to one of these places to lodge all night, in Gibeah, or in Ramah
man purposes his way, but providence leads even prudent choices into trial
a man's heart deviseth his way: but the LORD directeth his steps (Prov. 16:9)

schools.summary: Gill: they proceeded on their journey from near Jerusalem.
Ellicott: they were evidently reluctant, the sun going down upon them.

themes: the sun went down upon them when they were by Gibeah, which belongeth to Benjamin
the consequences of lost time gathering, providence bringing men to their appointed trial
ye know not what shall be on the morrow (James 4:14)

schools.summary: Gill: they turned out of their road into the city to seek a lodging; no man took them into his house.

themes: he sat him down in a street of the city: for there was no man that took them into his house to lodging
the want of hospitality marking a people fallen from love and the fear of God
I was a stranger, and ye took me not in (Matt. 25:43)

schools.summary: Gill: he came from his labour at even; he was of mount Ephraim, sojourning in Gibeah, but the men of the place were Benjamites.

themes: there came an old man from his work out of the field at even... of mount Ephraim... but the men of the place were Benjamites
grace dwelling in the stranger while the natives are corrupt; righteousness not bound to birth
a faithful man who can find? (Prov. 20:6)

schools.summary: Gill: coming into the city he looked up and saw the man.
Keil & Delitzsch: him asking, Whither goest thou? whence comest thou?

themes: the old man said, Whither goest thou? and whence comest thou?
having an eye for the stranger and the needy whom others pass by
look not every man on his own things, but every man also on the things of others (Phil. 2:4)

schools.summary: Gill: he answers the last question first; said, I am going to the house of the LORD, and no man receiveth me.

themes: I am now going to the house of the LORD; and there is no man that receiveth me to house
a people cold to God growing cold to his servants and the stranger
no man cared for my soul (Ps. 142:4)

schools.summary: Gill: he had straw to litter the asses and provender to feed them, brought with him.
Clarke: there was bread and provender, lacking nothing but lodging.

themes: there is both straw and provender for our asses; and there is bread and wine also for me... there is no want of any thing
considerate in one's need, content with little, unwilling to burden others
having food and raiment let us be therewith content (1 Tim. 6:8)

schools.summary: Gill: he wished him peace and tranquillity of mind, let nothing distress him.
Clarke: all thy wants lie upon me, the old man taking the charge.

themes: Peace be with thee; howsoever let all thy wants lie upon me; only lodge not in the street
the grace and duty of hospitality, freely receiving and providing for the stranger
be not forgetful to entertain strangers: for thereby some have entertained angels unawares (Heb. 13:2)

schools.summary: Gill: he showed him the way and in a kind and friendly manner bid him welcome.
Keil & Delitzsch: them washing their feet, eating, and drinking.

themes: So he brought him into his house... and they washed their feet, and did eat and drink
the duty of hospitality done heartily, refreshing the weary stranger
use hospitality one to another without grudging (1 Pet. 4:9)

schools.summary: Gill: they were making their hearts merry with a glass after supper.
Keil & Delitzsch: the sons of Belial besetting the house.

themes: the men of the city, certain sons of Belial, beset the house round about, and beat at the door
a professing people sunk to the very sin of Sodom where God is forsaken
the shew of their countenance doth witness against them; they declare their sin as Sodom (Isa. 3:9)

schools.summary: Gill: he went out to converse with them and reason with them.
Keil & Delitzsch: his plea, do not so wickedly.

themes: Nay, my brethren, nay, I pray you, do not so wickedly... do not this folly
the duty to withstand and reprove open sin even against a multitude
have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness, but rather reprove them (Eph. 5:11)

schools.summary: Gill: he offered his own daughter, a virgin, and the concubine of the Levite.
Keil & Delitzsch: the offer to spare the man.

debates: Was the old man's offer right or a sin?

The witnesses are clear the narrative records but does not approve it: like Lot at Sodom (Gen. 19:8), the old man proposed a real evil to prevent a worse, a grievous fault springing from fear and the lawless confusion of the times. Scripture sets it down as history, not as a pattern; we may never do evil that good may come (Rom. 3:8).
themes: here is my daughter a maiden, and his concubine; them I will bring out... but unto this man do not so vile a thing
a sinful doing of evil to prevent evil, recorded but not approved by Scripture
let us do evil, that good may come? whose damnation is just (Rom. 3:8)

schools.summary: Gill: the men would not hearken to him.
Clarke: the abuse continuing all the night until the morning.

themes: the men would not hearken to him... and they knew her, and abused her all the night until the morning
the savagery to which unbridled sin descends, the weak devoured without mercy
their feet are swift to shed blood... and the way of peace have they not known (Rom. 3:15-17)

schools.summary: Gill: the wicked who abused her had left her at dawn, at some distance; she fell down at the door where her lord was, till it was light.

themes: the woman... fell down at the door of the man's house where her lord was, till it was light
the bitterness of suffering wrought by men's sin upon the innocent
the LORD heareth the poor, and despiseth not his prisoners (Ps. 69:33)

schools.summary: Gill: it is scarcely reasonable to think he slept quietly after such a riot; he found her fallen at the door, her hands upon the threshold.

themes: the woman his concubine was fallen down at the door of the house, and her hands were upon the threshold
the fearful fruit of a nation's sin brought home to one household in grief
behold, and see if there be any sorrow like unto my sorrow (Lam. 1:12)

schools.summary: Gill: he spoke to her as supposing her asleep, to awake her; none answered, and he took her up upon the ass.

themes: Up, and let us be going. But none answered. Then the man took her up upon an ass... and gat him unto his place
the wages of sin death; the cruelty of the wicked crying to heaven for justice
the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground (Gen. 4:10)

schools.summary: Gill: he took the dead body, laid it in order, and divided it.
Clarke: he divided her and sent the pieces into all the coasts of Israel.

themes: he took a knife... and divided her... into twelve pieces, and sent her into all the coasts of Israel
great sin among God's people not to be hidden but brought to light to be judged
put away from among yourselves that wicked person (1 Cor. 5:13)

schools.summary: Gill: all that saw the pieces were made acquainted with the cause; they said, consider of it, take advice, and speak.

themes: There was no such deed done nor seen from the day that the children of Israel came up out of... Egypt... consider of it, take advice, and speak
sin crying out so that none can be silent; the call to weigh evil and judge it
consider your ways (Hag. 1:7)
